title: DRN Group
text:
Sheharyar Pervez (Condensed Matter | Computational Materials)  Email: sheharyar [at] giki.edu.pk 


I am a computational materials scientist working at the intersection of materials modeling, condensed matter physics, and data-driven discovery. My research combines density functional theory (DFT), molecular dynamics, and high-throughput computational approaches to understand and design advanced materials, with a particular focus on defects, electronic structure, and functional materials such as metal–organic frameworks and halide perovskites. I am also interested in the growing role of artificial intelligence and machine learning in accelerating materials research and scientific discovery.









Nasir Javed (Optoelectronics | Energy materials)  Email: nasir.javed [at] giki.edu.pk 


Dr. Nasir Javed’s current research interests include gaining a deeper understanding of the interaction between light and advanced functional optical materials at the nanoscale for implementation in optoelectronic devices, photonics, and organic optoelectronics. He conducts research into the optical and photophysical properties of nanostructured materials, with a particular emphasis on their applications in light-emitting devices and solar cells. His portfolio of materials is diverse and includes organic semiconductors, carbon nanostructures, nanocrystals, environmentally friendly materials, and piezoelectric materials. To achieve our research goals, we employ a range of experimental techniques for synthesizing, fabricating, and characterizing materials of interest alongside quantum chemistry simulation and machine learning algorithm for materials discovery.









Misbah Shaheen (Computational Materials | Condensed Matter)  Email: misbah.shaheen [at] giki.edu.pk 


I am a PhD researcher in computational materials science specializing in first-principles simulations based on Density Functional Theory (DFT). My research focuses on anion-exchanged and defect-engineered halide perovskites for next-generation optoelectronic and energy applications. My experience includes computational materials modeling, quantum mechanical simulations, and the use of high-performance computing techniques for materials design and analysis. My research interests further extend to advanced theoretical concepts including electron–phonon coupling, polaron formation, and low-dimensional as well as topological materials, with a broader aim of understanding and designing functional materials for emerging technologies.








Rao Uzair (Computational Materials | Condensed Matter)  











Naseeb Ullah (Computational Materials | Condensed Matter)

title: DRN Group
text:
How to choose a timestep (dt)?


The timestep must be much smaller than the fastest vibrations in your system.

If timestep is too large → atoms “overshoot” → energy drift, instability, or bonds break.
If timestep is too small → simulation is safe but unnecessarily slow.

Take the example of Halide Perovskite:
The fastest motions are bond stretching of Pb–Br / Pb–I / Cs–X and Br–Br / I–I interactions.
Typical bond vibrations are on the order of 10–100 THz (period ≈ 0.01–0.1 ps = 10–100 fs).
Rule of thumb: timestep ≲ 1/10 to 1/20 of the shortest vibrational period.
So:
If vibrational period ≈ 10 fs, then timestep ≲ 1 fs.
If vibrational period ≈ 20 fs, then timestep ≲ 2 fs.




How to choose a dumping factors?


For Nose-Hoover thermostat, typical choice for temp is 100 x dt and for pressure is 1000 x dt.




How to choose run value?


Use the formulas below:
\[simulated time required = \frac{\Delta T}{cooling rate}\]
\[number of steps = \frac{simulated time}{timestep}\]

title: LAMMPS GPU Installation
text:
LAMMPS Installation

Step 1
Install required dependencies:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install cmake build-essential git libfftw3-dev libjpeg-dev libpng-dev libopenmpi-dev
Also install CUDA if it’s not installed. Check with:
nvcc --version
nvidia-smi


Step 2
Clone and prepare the LAMMPS repository:
git clone https://github.com/lammps/lammps.git
cd lammps
mkdir build
cd build


Step 3 (Configure)
cmake ../cmake \
  -D CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=../lammps_gpu \
  -D BUILD_MPI=on \
  -D BUILD_OMP=on \
  -D PKG_KOKKOS=on \
  -D Kokkos_ENABLE_CUDA=on \
  -D Kokkos_ARCH_AMPERE80=on \
  -D CMAKE_C_COMPILER=mpicc \
  -D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=mpicxx
cmake ../cmake   -D CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=../lammps_gpu   -D CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release   -D BUILD_MPI=on   -D BUILD_OMP=on   -D CMAKE_C_COMPILER=mpicc   -D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=mpicxx   -D PKG_Kokkos=on   -D Kokkos_ENABLE_CUDA=on   -D Kokkos_ARCH_AMPERE80=on   -D PKG_GPU=on   -D PKG_USER-API=on   -D PKG_KSPACE=on   -D PKG_MANYBODY=on   -D PKG_MOLECULE=on   -D PKG_MISC=on   -D PKG_USER-REAXC=on   -D PKG_MLIAP=on   -D PKG_ML-SNAP=on   -D PKG_KIM=on   -D BIN2C=/home/misbah/nvhpc/Linux_x86_64/24.3/cuda/12.3/bin/bin2c


Step 4 (Build and Install)
make -j$(nproc)
make install

title: Tight-binding insights into ILHPs
text: Tight binding network

title: Tight-binding insights into ILHPs
text:
Background

The Tight-Binding Hamiltonian
The tight-binding method is a simple but powerful way to describe how electrons move in a solid. The core idea is that electrons tend to stay close to the atoms they belong to, but they can also spread out by “hopping” to neighboring atoms. This balance between staying localized and moving through the material, determines many of the electronic properties of a crystal.
By focusing only on the energy of an electron on an atom and the probability of hopping between nearby atoms, the tight-binding model captures the essential physics behind band formation, electrical conductivity, and the electronic structure of a wide range of materials. Despite its simplicity, it often provides accurate insights into systems ranging from common solids to advanced materials like graphene, semiconductors, and topological phases.


Anion-Exchance and ILHPs
Ionic liquid–hybrid perovskites (ILHPs) integrate ionic liquids into the perovskite lattice, enhancing stability, ion mobility, and defect behavior. Combined with anion exchange, which replaces halide ions (Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻) in the structure, these materials allow precise tuning of optical and electronic properties without altering the overall lattice.
Together, ILHPs and anion exchange provide a versatile platform for designing perovskites with tailored stability, bandgaps, and performance for applications in solar cells, LEDs, and other optoelectronic devices.

title: Tight-binding insights into ILHPs
text:
The Study

Our study employs a synergetic DFT and TB approach, to tune the bandgaps of cubic phase ILHPs (CsPbBr\(_x\) I\(_{3-x}\), x = 0, 1, 2, or 3) via anion exchange and create delocalization maps based on the TB Hamiltonians. Closer examination allows us to extract from this, reduced TB (redTB) models which are readily interpretable. The hopping distribution abstracted from the redTB model singles out CsPbBrI\(_2\) as being qualitatively and quantitatively different from the rest of the set, following a many-small as opposed to the few-big hopping scheme employed by the others.

title: Quantum Meets AI, 2026
text: Quantum Meets AI is 1+3 day event with an International Conference on Quantum Computing, followed by a 3-day workshop, DFT+ML, on the intersection of quantum materials and artificial intelligence. The workshop will cover the fundamentals of Density Functional Theory (DFT), machine learning techniques for materials science, and cheminformatics approaches for representing chemical data.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
section: Welcome to the Frontier: Where Science Meets AI
text:
Welcome to the Frontier: Where Science Meets AI

Have you ever wondered how a computer actually “sees” a molecule?
Can an algorithm predict the next breakthrough solar cell or smartphone battery faster than a scientist in a lab coat?
What happens when we teach the laws of quantum physics to an Artificial Intelligence?

If you are a physicist or chemist looking to scale up your discoveries, or a data scientist looking for complex, real-world problems to solve, you are in the right place.
In what follows we give an insight into how these questions are answered in the world of matter modeling. We will go into greater depth during the workshop.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
section: Part 1: What is Computational Materials Science?
text:
Part 1: What is Computational Materials Science?
At its core, computational materials science is about simulating reality.
Instead of mixing chemicals in a traditional laboratory, researchers use powerful computers to build virtual models of atoms and molecules. By applying the laws of physics to these digital models, we can predict how a material will behave—how strong it is, how it conducts electricity, or how it reacts to light—before it is ever physically created.

The Traditional Bottleneck
Historically, discovering a new material takes about 10 to 20 years. It involves a lot of trial and error: cook a sample in a lab, test it, fail, tweak the recipe, and try again. Computational modeling shortens this loop by letting us screen thousands of virtual candidates first, picking only the absolute best ones to actually manufacture in the real world.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
section: Part 2: The Workshop Journey (Day by Day)
text:
Part 2: The Workshop Journey (Day by Day)
To understand how we accelerate this science, our workshop is broken into three core pillars:

🔬 Day 1: Zooming In with Quantum Mechanics (DFT)
Before we can use AI, we need high-quality data. That data comes from the fundamental laws of physics. On Day 1, we look at Density Functional Theory (DFT).

The Concept: Think of DFT as a super-powered, quantum-mechanical microscope. Everything in the universe is made of atoms, and how those atoms behave depends entirely on their electrons. DFT is a mathematical cleverness that lets computers calculate exactly where electrons want to go and how they hold atoms together.
Why it matters: It gives us the “ground truth” physics of a material. If you want to know if a crystal will be stable or what color light a semiconductor will absorb, DFT gives you the precise answer from first principles.



🤖 Day 2: Scaling Up with Artificial Intelligence (AI)
If DFT is so accurate, why do we need AI? Because quantum mechanics is incredibly slow. Calculating the properties of just a few hundred atoms using DFT can take a supercomputer days or weeks.

The Concept: Instead of running a slow physics calculation for every single new material, we use Machine Learning. We take the results of thousands of previous DFT calculations and use them to train an AI.
The AI’s Job: The AI learns the underlying patterns—the “rules of thumb” of how atoms interact. Once trained, the AI can predict the properties of a brand-new material in milliseconds rather than days.



🧬 Day 3: Talking to Computers (Cheminformatics & Graph Neural Networks)
To train an AI on materials, we have a unique challenge: How do you describe a molecule or a crystal to a computer? You can’t just type in “this looks like a shiny metal.”

Cheminformatics: This is the language interface. It converts chemical structures, formulas, and bonds into digital data strings (like code) that a computer can read, sort, and search.
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs): This is where modern AI gets beautiful. In physics and chemistry, molecules and crystals are essentially networks of atoms connected by bonds. In computer science, this is called a Graph (nodes connected by lines). GNNs are a special type of AI designed specifically to look at these chemical networks, understanding how the arrangement and neighborhood of each atom dictate the ultimate property of the entire material.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
section: Part 3: Why This Interdisciplinary Mix Matters
text:
Part 3: Why This Interdisciplinary Mix Matters
This workshop brings together two completely different ways of thinking, and that is where the magic happens:

For the Physicists, Chemists, and Materials Scientists: You bring the intuition, the domain expertise, and the fundamental laws of nature. AI gives you a digital superpower to scale your insights from a few molecules to millions of possibilities overnight.
For the Computer and Data Scientists: You bring the algorithms, the pattern-recognition tools, and the data architecture. Materials science gives you some of the richest, most complex, and physically meaningful datasets in the world to test and stretch your models.

Together, we aren’t just predicting the future of technology—we are computing the very matter it will be built from.

title: DRN Group
text:
SCF calculations solve many one particle Schrodinger like KS equation (eq. num) for a given system. This problem is called self consistent because Hartee and exchange-correlation potential in KS equation require the electron density which require wave function \(\psi\) to obtain the solution to the equation. Or simple, a wavefunction \(\psi_i\) is dependent on all the other solutions \(\psi_j\) describing the electronic states. Therefore, SCF calculation is an itterative process and solve the equations numerically.
Practically, nuclear potential is calculated by giving the atomic site coordinates and \(V_H\) and \(V_XC\) are calculated using the initial guess of electron density. This way, the total potential is calculated which is used by KS equations to calculate wavefunction and then obtain the electron density. The process is repeated until the value of electron density converges to obtain the ground state energy. Flow chart below shows the cyclic process of SCF calculations.



SCF Flow Diagram

title: DRN Group
text:
SCF calculations solve many one particle Schrodinger like KS equation (eq. num) for a given system. This problem is called self consistent because Hartee and exchange-correlation potential in KS equation require the electron density which require wave function \(\psi\) to obtain the solution to the equation. Or simple, a wavefunction \(\psi_i\) is dependent on all the other solutions \(\psi_j\) describing the electronic states. Therefore, SCF calculation is an itterative process and solve the equations numerically.
Practically, nuclear potential is calculated by giving the atomic site coordinates and \(V_H\) and \(V_XC\) are calculated using the initial guess of electron density. This way, the total potential is calculated which is used by KS equations to calculate wavefunction and then obtain the electron density. The process is repeated until the value of electron density converges to obtain the ground state energy. Flow chart below shows the cyclic process of SCF calculations.



SCF Flow Diagram

title: DRN Group
text:
Calculations
SCF calculations give the total energy of the system to obtain the ground state properties of material. But prior to scf calculations, relaxation calculation is necessary to remove the strains on the system and optimize the atomic positions as well as unit cell. QE uses BFGS algorithm (Do i need to explain BFGS theorem?) to optimize the atomic positions through relax and cell parameters through vc-relax calculation. It is itterative process similar to scf calculations where BFGS algorithm obtain the values of atomic positions and then energy is calculated through SCF calculation and forces are calculated using Hellmann‐Feynman theorem. The cycle stops when the energy and forces values achieve convergence criteris provided in the input file. Sample files of relax, vc-relax and scf calculation can be found at:

relax.in
vc-relax.in
scf.in

title: DRN Group
text:
Run Calculations
QE uses pw.x code to run the above calculations. The difference is only in the input files. First run relx/vcrelax and obtain the corresponding optimized atomicc positions and cell parameters. Then perform convergence test and finaly perform the SCF calculation with optimized parameters.
mpirun -np 4 pw.x < relax.in > relax.out
mpirun -np 4 pw.x < vcrelax.in > vcrelax.out
mpirun -np 4 pw.x < scf.in > scf.out

title: DRN Group
text:
Convergence in SCF calculations
Convergence test should be perform on energ cutoff and k-mesh after structure relaxation as shown below in graph to obtain the correct parameters. For this purpose, perform multiple scf calculations for different values.
The energy cutoff determines how many plane waves are to be used for calculations which is directly related to the accuracy of results. However, it is to keep in mind that higher energy cutoff increases the computational cost. Mostly pseudopotential files provide the estimated energy cutoff but it is still recommended to perform convergence test. Ultrasoft pseudopotentials suggest low energy cutoff while it is larger for norm conserving pseudopotentials.
k-mesh convergence can be performed by changing the k points in the input file of QE. There are six values: first 3 values are number of k-points and the last 3 values represent lattice shifting in each direction which can take value either 0 or 1.

title: Kohn-Sham States and the Number of Electrons
text:
If you open the header of an espresso pw.x output file, you might see:
     number of electrons       =        44.00
     number of Kohn-Sham states=           26
Well, what are these?
In understanding electronic structure calculations using density functional theory (DFT), it’s useful to imagine a familiar situation: a family going on a picnic.



The number of KS States is like the rows of seats on a bus and the number of electrons are akin to the people riding out to picnic.


The number of valence electrons in a material is like the number of family members who are actually going on the picnic. These are the people who will participate in activities, contribute to the experience, and need to be accounted for in the logistics. Similarly, valence electrons are those that participate in bonding and electronic behavior, and are the focus of most DFT calculations.
However, in organizing a real trip, one often brings more than just the family. There may be a hired guide to navigate, porters to carry supplies, or someone skilled in negotiation for dealing with vendors. This expanded group represents the total number of electrons in a DFT calculation. It includes both the valence electrons and any additional electrons that the pseudopotential has chosen to treat explicitly—perhaps semicore states or shallow core levels that are important for accuracy in certain elements.
To transport this group, a bus is needed. The Kohn–Sham states correspond to the rows of seats on the bus. In a non-spin-polarized system, each row can seat two people: one with spin up and one with spin down. Therefore, the number of rows ideally needs to be at least half the number of electrons. But planning conservatively, we often include more rows than necessary—not everyone likes to share a seat, and we may want to observe what other passengers (electronic states) are doing even if they are not part of the initial group. These extra rows represent the unoccupied Kohn–Sham states, which are important for understanding conduction bands, optical transitions, and other excited-state properties.
This analogy helps clarify why the number of Kohn–Sham states is not the same as the number of electrons, and why pseudopotentials and spin considerations affect how many orbitals the DFT calculation must solve for.
While the picnic bus analogy helps build intuition about valence electrons, total electrons, and Kohn–Sham states in DFT, it necessarily simplifies a number of important physical and computational subtleties and its limitations must always be at the back of our mind:

First, the analogy treats Kohn–Sham states as fixed seats, suggesting a discrete, localized picture. In reality, Kohn–Sham orbitals are continuous wavefunctions—often delocalized over an entire crystal in the form of Bloch states. They are not static slots that electrons occupy, but mathematical solutions to a differential equation that describe how electron density is distributed in space.
Second, the analogy implies that electrons either fully occupy or do not occupy Kohn–Sham states, like passengers sitting in seats. This works for insulators and simple semiconductors, but breaks down in metals or finite-temperature systems where fractional occupations are common. In such cases, the occupancy of a state is not binary, but smoothly distributed around the Fermi level according to the Fermi–Dirac distribution.
Third, unoccupied Kohn–Sham states are portrayed as empty rows included just in case they’re needed. However, these states are not merely optional extras—they are integral to predicting important physical properties, such as optical absorption spectra, band gaps, and excited-state behavior. They must be computed carefully and with the same rigor as occupied states.
Fourth, the treatment of spin in the analogy is simplified. While saying that “some people prefer not to share a row” illustrates the difference between spin-polarized and non-spin-polarized calculations, it glosses over more subtle aspects of spin, such as antiferromagnetic ordering, spin splitting, and spin–orbit coupling. These effects can lead to highly nontrivial behavior in the electronic structure, which cannot be captured by the seating metaphor.
Finally, while the role of the pseudopotential is likened to choosing who goes on the trip, the analogy omits the fact that pseudopotentials also encode how valence electrons interact with the frozen core. This interaction affects transferability, scattering properties, and the accuracy of calculated forces—factors that are invisible in the analogy.

text:
&CONTROL
  calculation = 'scf', !fe fn 
  prefix = 'si',
  outdir = './tmp/',
  pseudo_dir = './pseudo/',
  verbosity = 'high',
  tprnfor = .true.,
  tstress = .true.,
/

&SYSTEM
  ibrav = 2,
  celldm(1) = 10.2,
  nat = 2,
  ntyp = 1,
  ecutwfc = 40.0,
  ecutrho = 320.0,
/

&ELECTRONS
  conv_thr = 1.0d-8,
  mixing_beta = 0.7,
/

ATOMIC_SPECIES
  Si  28.0855  Si.pz-vbc.UPF

ATOMIC_POSITIONS crystal
  Si 0.00 0.00 0.00
  Si 0.25 0.25 0.25

K_POINTS automatic
  6 6 6  1 1 1

title: Epicurus and Greek Atomism
text:
Epicurus was a key figure in the history of atomism. He revived and transformed the atomism of Democritus into a comprehensive philosophy of nature grounded in materialism and natural causation. By introducing the concept of the atomic “swerve,” he softened strict determinism and connected atomism to human freedom and ethical life.

Epicurus believed in an eternal universe, where anything that comes into being must come from something that exists, all that is destroyed does not cease to exist, but all that exists always will.He held that this universe consists of two things; matter and void.[11] Matter is made up of atoms, tiny bodies that are unable to be broken down into smaller parts, that only have the unchanging qualities of shape, size, and weight. There are an infinite number of these atoms, though only a finite number of types, and an infinite amount of void. Because of the infinite supply of atoms, there are an infinite number of worlds, each separated from each other by vast areas of void, some of which could be vastly different from our own.


The atoms are in constant motion through the void, moving in one of four different ways. They can collide with each other, either bouncing back, or joining together and vibrating to maintain the overall shape of the larger object.[14] When not prevented by other atoms, all atoms naturally move at the same speed downwards in relation to the rest of the world, though they must also occasionally “swerve” randomly out of their usual path; without the swerving motion, atoms would never collide with each other from their parallel trajectory downwards.[15] Epicurus argued that the swerve, which was absent in Democritus’ earlier atomic theory, was also what accounted for humanity’s free will; if it were not for the swerve, humans would be subject to a never-ending chain of cause and effect.


Epicurus also believed that senses relied on atoms; which were constantly being emitted from every object. Atoms themselves, which only had size, shape, and weight, did not possess the qualities perceived by the senses, such as redness, but instead would cause the observer to experience them in their mind. Because atoms moved sufficiently quickly, this would be experienced as a continuous sensation of vision.

Source: Wikipedia/Epicurus

title: About
text:
DRN is a research group active at GIK Institute. As mentioned on the home page, the name is a nod to Lucretius’ poem on the same name, which sought to explain the universe through the lens of atomism and reason, free from superstition.
The DRN group studies properties of matter in the computational realm. Our work lies at the intersection of physics, chemistry, and data science—leveraging first-principles simulations, high-throughput workflows, and machine learning to advance materials discovery. Rooted in open science and guided by curiosity, we build tools, datasets, and models that make this understanding widely accessible.
Email: drn@giki.edu.pk

title: Atomism: Ancient to Modern
text:
We believe we owe a debt to history. In these pages we shall go over the history of atomism, from its origins in ancient Greece to its more modern forms.

title: Defects in Perovskites
text: Perovskites are a fascinating class of materials known for their remarkable ability to convert sunlight into electricity and emit bright, tunable light. But like any material, they are not perfect—tiny imperfections, or defects, naturally appear in their crystal structure. The presence of transition metals adds another layer of complexity, as their multiple oxidation states and strong interactions with the lattice can create additional defect states or alter defect behavior. Some defects can improve performance, enhancing how electrons move or how light is absorbed, while others act as traps that reduce efficiency and stability. By studying these defects i.e. how they form, migrate, and interact, we can better understand perovskites and design materials that are more efficient, longer-lasting, and tailored for specific applications.

title: Defects in Perovskites
text: Perovskites are a fascinating class of materials known for their remarkable ability to convert sunlight into electricity and emit bright, tunable light. But like any material, they are not perfect—tiny imperfections, or defects, naturally appear in their crystal structure. The presence of transition metals adds another layer of complexity, as their multiple oxidation states and strong interactions with the lattice can create additional defect states or alter defect behavior. Some defects can improve performance, enhancing how electrons move or how light is absorbed, while others act as traps that reduce efficiency and stability. By studying these defects i.e. how they form, migrate, and interact, we can better understand perovskites and design materials that are more efficient, longer-lasting, and tailored for specific applications.

title: DRN Group
text:
Lucretius







Nothing can be created out of nothing.





De Rerum Natura—Latin for On the Nature of Things  —takes its name from Lucretius’ famous poem, which sought to explain the universe through the lens of atomism and reason, free from superstition.
At DRN we are concerned with the nature of things as well, albeit in the computational realm. We explore matter and its methods, through the lens of simulations and data. Our work lies at the intersection of physics, chemistry, and data science—leveraging first-principles simulations, high-throughput workflows, and machine learning to advance materials discovery. Rooted in open science and guided by curiosity, we build tools, datasets, and models that make this understanding widely accessible.
Thus when the mood is less poetic, we explain DRN as being:  Data-driven Research in Nanomaterials.

3. Pervez, S. & Iqbal, M. Z. Evaluation of battery-grade alkaline earth metal sulfide electrodes for energy storage applications. International Journal of Energy Research 46, 8093–8101 (2022).

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
text:
What is Computational Materials Science?
At its core, computational materials science is about simulating reality.
Instead of mixing chemicals in a traditional laboratory, researchers use powerful computers to build virtual models of atoms and molecules. By applying the laws of physics to these digital models, we can predict how a material will behave—how strong it is, how it conducts electricity, or how it reacts to light—before it is ever physically created.

The Traditional Bottleneck
Historically, discovering a new material takes about 10 to 20 years. It involves a lot of trial and error: cook a sample in a lab, test it, fail, tweak the recipe, and try again. Computational modeling shortens this loop by letting us screen thousands of virtual candidates first, picking only the absolute best ones to actually manufacture in the real world.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
text:
The Workshop Journey (Day by Day)
To understand how we accelerate this science, our workshop is broken into three core pillars:

🔬 Day 1: Zooming In with Quantum Mechanics (DFT)
Before we can use AI, we need high-quality data. That data comes from the fundamental laws of physics. On Day 1, we look at Density Functional Theory (DFT).

The Concept: Think of DFT as a super-powered, quantum-mechanical microscope. Everything in the universe is made of atoms, and how those atoms behave depends entirely on their electrons. DFT is a mathematical cleverness that lets computers calculate exactly where electrons want to go and how they hold atoms together.
Why it matters: It gives us the “ground truth” physics of a material. If you want to know if a crystal will be stable or what color light a semiconductor will absorb, DFT gives you the precise answer from first principles.



🤖 Day 2: Scaling Up with Artificial Intelligence (AI)
If DFT is so accurate, why do we need AI? Because quantum mechanics is incredibly slow. Calculating the properties of just a few hundred atoms using DFT can take a supercomputer days or weeks.

The Concept: Instead of running a slow physics calculation for every single new material, we use Machine Learning. We take the results of thousands of previous DFT calculations and use them to train an AI.
The AI’s Job: The AI learns the underlying patterns—the “rules of thumb” of how atoms interact. Once trained, the AI can predict the properties of a brand-new material in milliseconds rather than days.



🧬 Day 3: Talking to Computers (Cheminformatics & Graph Neural Networks)
To train an AI on materials, we have a unique challenge: How do you describe a molecule or a crystal to a computer? You can’t just type in “this looks like a shiny metal.”

Cheminformatics: This is the language interface. It converts chemical structures, formulas, and bonds into digital data strings (like code) that a computer can read, sort, and search.
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs): This is where modern AI gets beautiful. In physics and chemistry, molecules and crystals are essentially networks of atoms connected by bonds. In computer science, this is called a Graph (nodes connected by lines). GNNs are a special type of AI designed specifically to look at these chemical networks, understanding how the arrangement and neighborhood of each atom dictate the ultimate property of the entire material.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
text:
Why This Interdisciplinary Mix Matters
This workshop brings together two completely different ways of thinking, and that is where the magic happens:

For the Physicists, Chemists, and Materials Scientists: You bring the intuition, the domain expertise, and the fundamental laws of nature. AI gives you a digital superpower to scale your insights from a few molecules to millions of possibilities overnight.
For the Computer and Data Scientists: You bring the algorithms, the pattern-recognition tools, and the data architecture. Materials science gives you some of the richest, most complex, and physically meaningful datasets in the world to test and stretch your models.

Together, we aren’t just predicting the future of technology—we are computing the very matter it will be built from.

title: Quantum Meets AI International Conference 2026
text:
Conference dates: July 13, 2026 Venue: Ghulam Ishaq Khan Institute of Information Technology, Topi, Pakistan
The conference will bring together students, researchers, academics, and professionals to explore recent advances in quantum computing and emerging quantum technologies. Participants will gain practical insight into how the field is moving from fundamental research toward real-world applications through expert talks, experimental perspectives, and selected insights from industry professionals. The event will also highlight current challenges, future opportunities, and provide a valuable platform for networking and collaboration within the quantum ecosystem.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
section: Welcome to the Frontier: Where Science Meets AI
text:
Welcome to the Frontier: Where Science Meets AI

Have you ever wondered how a computer actually “sees” a molecule?
Can an algorithm predict the next breakthrough solar cell or smartphone battery faster than a scientist in a lab coat?
What happens when we teach the laws of quantum physics to an Artificial Intelligence?

If you are a physicist or chemist looking to scale up your discoveries, or a data scientist looking for complex, real-world problems to solve, you are in the right place.
In what follows we give an insight into how these questions are answered in the world of matter modeling. We will go into greater depth during the workshop.

title: Quantum Meets AI Workshop 2026
text:
